{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Subscription billing platform managing lifecycle states (activation, renewal, cancellation) with tiered policies and trial management. The rules govern payment validation, renewal timing, failure handling, reactivation policy, and trial abuse prevention.",
    "bounded_context": "Subscription Billing & Lifecycle Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Subscription", "definition": "A recurring billing agreement between the platform and a customer, with a defined billing cycle and tier." },
      { "term": "Payment Method", "definition": "A stored instrument (card, bank account) used to collect recurring charges. Has a status (valid, failed, expired)." },
      { "term": "Billing Cycle", "definition": "The recurring time period (e.g., monthly, annual) that defines when charges occur." },
      { "term": "Renewal", "definition": "The process of charging the customer and extending the subscription for another billing cycle." },
      { "term": "Renewal Attempt", "definition": "A single try to process the renewal payment. Failed attempts are counted toward cancellation thresholds." },
      { "term": "Grace Period", "definition": "A time window after payment failure during which the subscription remains active before cancellation." },
      { "term": "Premium Tier", "definition": "A subscription level that receives preferential treatment in failure handling (14-day grace period)." },
      { "term": "Free Trial", "definition": "A zero-cost subscription period offered to new customers, limited to one active trial per email address." },
      { "term": "Cancelled Subscription", "definition": "A terminal state from which a subscription cannot be reactivated. The customer must create a new subscription." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A subscription cannot be activated with a failed payment method on file.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a state integrity constraint. Activating a subscription with a known-bad payment method means the system has entered a corrupt state — it has promised service delivery backed by a payment instrument it already knows cannot collect revenue. This is not a policy preference; it is a precondition for the subscription object to be valid. If violated, the system is selling service it cannot bill for, creating immediate financial exposure and a data integrity problem (active subscription with no viable funding source).",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: For any subscription transitioning to ACTIVE state, the associated payment method's status MUST NOT be FAILED at the moment of activation."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Subscriptions auto-renew 7 days before the billing cycle ends to allow retry time.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The 7-day lead time is a business policy decision — a tunable window chosen to balance retry opportunity against premature charges. A VP could reasonably change this to 5 days or 10 days based on payment processor SLAs, customer feedback, or retry success rate data. The system is not corrupt if renewal is attempted 5 days before instead of 7. The concept of 'renew early enough to allow retries' is sound, but the specific number is a configurable threshold, not a physical or integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: Renewal processing initiates exactly 7 days before the billing cycle end date."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "After 3 failed renewal attempts, the subscription is automatically cancelled.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The 3-attempt threshold is a business policy balancing customer retention against revenue risk. A VP could change this to 2 or 5 attempts based on churn analysis, payment processor costs per retry, or competitive benchmarking. The underlying principle — 'cancel after exhausting retries' — is important, but the specific count of 3 is a tunable parameter. The system is not in a corrupt state if it retries 4 times instead of 3; it has merely applied a different policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: When a non-Premium subscription accumulates 3 consecutive failed renewal attempts, its status transitions to CANCELLED."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "A cancelled subscription cannot be reactivated — customers must create a new subscription.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a state machine integrity constraint. CANCELLED is a terminal state. If a cancelled subscription could be reactivated, the system loses auditability — billing history, cancellation reasons, trial tracking (BR-6), and revenue recognition all depend on the guarantee that a cancelled subscription's history is sealed. Allowing reactivation would corrupt the subscription lifecycle state machine, break audit trails, and potentially circumvent trial-per-email limits. This is not a policy preference; it is a structural invariant of the domain model.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: No state transition exists from CANCELLED to ACTIVE (or any non-terminal state). Any attempt to reactivate a cancelled subscription MUST be rejected."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Premium tier subscribers get a 14-day grace period before cancellation instead of the 3-strike rule.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The 14-day grace period is a tiered business policy designed to retain higher-value customers. A VP could change this to 21 days or 10 days based on premium churn data, LTV calculations, or competitive positioning. The concept of 'premium customers get more lenient failure handling' is a strategic choice, and the specific duration is a configurable parameter. The system is not corrupt if the grace period is 10 days instead of 14.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: When a Premium tier subscription's renewal payment fails, the subscription remains active for 14 calendar days before transitioning to CANCELLED (regardless of retry attempt count within that window)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Free trial subscriptions cannot have more than one active trial per email address at any time.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a data consistency and anti-abuse constraint. If an email address has two active trials simultaneously, the system has entered a corrupt state — it has failed to enforce a uniqueness constraint that protects revenue integrity. This is not about how many trials are 'preferred'; it is a hard constraint that, if violated, means the trial-gating logic is broken. It is analogous to a unique constraint in a database — the system's data is inconsistent if two active trials coexist for the same email. Additionally, this directly protects against trial abuse, which is a financial integrity concern.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: At any point in time, COUNT of subscriptions WHERE type=FREE_TRIAL AND status=ACTIVE AND email=X MUST be <= 1. Any attempt to create a second active trial for the same email MUST be rejected."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A customer attempts to activate a subscription",
      "when": "The payment method on file has a FAILED status",
      "then": "The activation is rejected and the subscription remains in its pre-active state with an error indicating payment method must be resolved"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "An active subscription with a billing cycle ending on date D",
      "when": "The current date reaches D minus 7 days",
      "then": "The system initiates the renewal payment process automatically"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A non-Premium active subscription with 2 prior failed renewal attempts",
      "when": "The third renewal attempt fails",
      "then": "The subscription status transitions to CANCELLED automatically"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A subscription in CANCELLED status",
      "when": "Any actor attempts to reactivate it (via API, UI, or internal process)",
      "then": "The reactivation is rejected; the system directs the customer to create a new subscription"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A Premium tier subscription whose renewal payment has failed",
      "when": "14 calendar days elapse from the first failure without a successful payment",
      "then": "The subscription transitions to CANCELLED"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A Premium tier subscription whose renewal payment has failed",
      "when": "A successful payment is processed within the 14-day grace period",
      "then": "The subscription remains ACTIVE and the grace period resets"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A customer with an active free trial subscription",
      "when": "They attempt to create another free trial subscription using the same email address",
      "then": "The creation is rejected with an error indicating an active trial already exists for that email"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Payment method fails between validation and activation (race condition)", "expected_behavior": "Activation must re-validate payment method status at the moment of state transition, not rely on a prior check." },
    { "scenario": "Premium subscriber downgrades to standard tier during the 14-day grace period", "expected_behavior": "Spec is ambiguous — does the grace period survive the downgrade, or does the 3-strike rule immediately apply? Requires clarification." },
    { "scenario": "Customer has a cancelled trial and attempts a new trial with the same email", "expected_behavior": "Should be ALLOWED — the rule restricts concurrent active trials, not lifetime trial count. However, the spec does not explicitly address lifetime limits." },
    { "scenario": "Renewal attempt #3 fails but payment succeeds on a manual retry before cancellation is processed", "expected_behavior": "Timing matters — if cancellation is asynchronous, a narrow window may exist where manual payment saves the subscription. Needs defined ordering." },
    { "scenario": "Email alias or '+' addressing used to circumvent one-trial-per-email rule", "expected_behavior": "Spec says 'per email address' — must decide whether to normalize emails (strip aliases, case-fold) or enforce literally." },
    { "scenario": "Premium subscriber's grace period overlaps with the next billing cycle", "expected_behavior": "Must define whether the grace period pauses the billing cycle clock or runs independently alongside it." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "These rules directly govern revenue collection. BR-1 prevents revenue leakage from unfunded subscriptions. BR-3 and BR-5 balance customer retention against carrying non-paying subscribers. BR-6 prevents trial abuse that erodes conversion funnel economics.",
    "operational": "The 7-day renewal lead time (BR-2) creates a retry window that reduces involuntary churn and support ticket volume. The Premium grace period (BR-5) requires a separate timer-based workflow distinct from the standard attempt-counter, adding operational complexity.",
    "user": "The cancellation-is-terminal rule (BR-4) creates friction for returning customers who must re-onboard. The Premium grace period (BR-5) differentiates the premium experience and reduces churn anxiety for high-value customers.",
    "risk": "BR-4's terminal cancellation is the highest-risk rule from a customer satisfaction perspective — a wrongful cancellation (e.g., due to a payment processor outage) cannot be undone. BR-6 enforcement depends on email normalization strategy, which is an abuse vector if not addressed.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers (Standard)", "impact": "Subject to 3-strike cancellation with no reactivation path; must re-subscribe if cancelled." },
      { "group": "Customers (Premium)", "impact": "Receive more lenient 14-day grace period; higher retention protection." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Depends on accurate enforcement of all rules for revenue recognition, churn metrics, and trial conversion tracking." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will handle complaints from customers who cannot reactivate cancelled subscriptions; needs clear policy documentation." },
      { "group": "Product/Growth Team", "impact": "Trial abuse prevention (BR-6) directly affects funnel metrics and CAC calculations." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 72,
    "reasoning": "The six rules are clearly stated and internally consistent for their core behaviors. However, several interaction effects between rules are unspecified (Premium downgrade during grace period, grace period vs billing cycle overlap, email normalization for trial uniqueness). The invariant classifications are high-confidence, but complete acceptance criteria depend on resolving the identified edge cases.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Invariant classifications for all six rules",
      "BR-1: Activation precondition is unambiguous",
      "BR-4: Terminal cancellation state is clearly a state machine invariant",
      "BR-6: One-active-trial-per-email is a uniqueness constraint"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Interaction between Premium grace period and tier changes",
      "Email normalization strategy for trial uniqueness enforcement",
      "Timing semantics when cancellation races with manual payment",
      "Whether Premium grace period and billing cycle clock are independent"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "CANCELLED is the only terminal state in the subscription lifecycle",
      "The 3-strike rule applies per renewal cycle, not across the subscription lifetime",
      "Premium 14-day grace period replaces (not supplements) the 3-strike mechanism",
      "One-trial-per-email applies to concurrently active trials, not lifetime trial count"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Premium tier grace period interaction with tier changes: if a Premium subscriber downgrades to Standard during the 14-day grace period, does the grace period continue or does the 3-strike rule immediately take effect?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers (Premium)", "Customer Support", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["Grace period survives downgrade (honor the original tier at time of failure)", "3-strike rule applies immediately upon downgrade", "Grace period converts to remaining-attempt equivalent"],
      "recommendation": "Honor the grace period that was in effect when the failure occurred — changing rules mid-failure-recovery punishes the customer and creates support escalations."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Email normalization for trial uniqueness: does 'per email address' mean exact string match, or should the system normalize (case-fold, strip Gmail-style '+' aliases, etc.)?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product/Growth Team", "Customers", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["Exact string match (simple but easily circumvented)", "Case-insensitive match (minimal normalization)", "Full normalization including alias stripping (strongest abuse prevention)"],
      "recommendation": "At minimum, case-insensitive matching. Alias stripping depends on abuse data — start with case-fold and monitor."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Race condition between automatic cancellation after 3 failures and a successful manual payment: if the customer pays manually after the 3rd failure but before the cancellation job executes, which wins?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers (Standard)", "Customer Support", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["Cancellation always wins (3 failures = cancelled, period)", "Successful payment within a defined window prevents cancellation", "Cancellation is synchronous with the 3rd failure, eliminating the race"],
      "recommendation": "Make cancellation synchronous with the 3rd failure event to eliminate the race entirely. If async, a successful payment before cancellation executes should prevent it — punishing a customer who just paid is a terrible experience."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
